topicArea: Philosophy of Humor, Competing Theories of Laughter & the Search for a Single Essence

questions.passage:
It is commonly said that explaining a joke kills it, and the observation is usually offered as a warning to bores. It might better be read as a datum. Something about humor resists the very operation, articulate the conditions, state the mechanism, that philosophy performs on its objects, and the long record of philosophical attempts to say what makes things funny is largely a record of theories that work beautifully until they meet the next counterexample. Three families of theories have dominated that record, and their instructive failures may say as much about theorizing as about laughter.

The oldest family locates humor in superiority. On this account, laughter is a small triumph: we laugh at the misfortune, folly, or deformity of others, enjoying a sudden consciousness of our own eminence by comparison. The theory has no trouble with mockery, satire, or the pratfall, and it explains the ancient moralists' suspicion of laughter as an essentially uncharitable pleasure. Its troubles begin with the humor that involves no victim at all. A pun victimizes no one; a purely absurd juxtaposition elevates the laugher over nobody; and people demonstrably laugh, with warmth rather than contempt, at their own harmless blunders. A theory that must strain to find a defeated party in every wordplay has begun to look like a theory defending itself rather than explaining its subject.

A second family treats laughter as discharge: the venting, through a safe physical channel, of nervous energy summoned for some purpose and then left without employment. The account had the prestige of a hydraulic psychology behind it, and it retains an insight the others lack, a feel for timing, for the way laughter erupts precisely when built-up tension is suddenly released, as when a moment of suspense resolves into harmlessness. But the underlying hydraulics have not survived as science, and the theory drifts toward circularity when pressed for detail: asked why some releases of tension amuse while others merely relieve, it can usually answer only that the amusing ones were of the amusing kind.

The third family, dominant today, finds humor in incongruity: the perception of a mismatch between what a situation leads us to expect and what it delivers, enjoyed rather than suffered. The theory covers an impressive range, puns turn on incongruous double meanings, absurdist comedy on violated logic, wit on the collision of remote ideas, and it links humor to cognition in a way that invites empirical study. Yet incongruity alone is plainly insufficient. A stranger at the door where a friend was expected is incongruous and merely unsettling; a sudden discrepancy in one's bank balance is incongruous and alarming. Sensing this, the theory's defenders add qualifiers, the incongruity must be resolved, or playful, or benign, and here a familiar trouble reappears, since benign threatens to mean nothing more determinate than such as to amuse, at which point the theory explains the funny by the funny.

One response to this record of near-misses is to keep refining. Another is to suspect the enterprise. The three families may each have hold of a genuine strand, superiority of much ridicule, release of comic timing, incongruity of wit, without there being any single property that all funny things share and all unfunny things lack. On this view humor is a family-resemblance concept: instances overlap and crisscross like the members of a family, alike in various ways, without one feature running through the whole. The search for humor's essence would then be not incomplete but misconceived, and the counterexample mill would be permanent, since every proposed essence must fail somewhere on so heterogeneous a terrain.

What a definitional approach also obscures, some argue, is what laughter does between people. Laughter is overwhelmingly social, rare in solitude, contagious in groups, and it functions as a signal: shared laughter marks shared assumptions, tests them, welcomes insiders, and, less comfortably, marks the line where outsiders stand. This would explain at once why humor varies so radically across cultures and generations, the assumptions being tested differ, and why it exists everywhere, the need to test and signal them does not. The social account does not answer the definitional question so much as demote it, suggesting that what unifies humor may be its work rather than its content.

None of this licenses the conclusion that nothing general can be said. Each classical theory earns its keep within a region of the terrain, and the mapping of regions is genuine knowledge. What the record counsels is suspicion of a particular ambition, the single formula, adequate everywhere, that the subject has now declined to yield for a very long time, under sustained and intelligent pressure. Perhaps the joke about explanation killing the joke was pointing at something real: not that analysis is forbidden, but that this phenomenon, woven into play, surprise, and the shifting boundaries of social life, was never the kind of thing a one-line essence was going to capture.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The incongruity theory has definitively superseded the superiority and relief theories as the correct account of humor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The three classic theories' failures suggest humor lacks a single essence, though each captures a genuine region of the phenomenon.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Laughter is fundamentally an uncharitable pleasure taken in others' defects, which explains why moralists have long been suspicious of it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because humor varies so radically across cultures, no theory of any kind can say anything true about it.

explanations.A: This choice mistakes present dominance for vindication. The passage calls incongruity theory dominant today but immediately shows it insufficient, incongruity can unsettle or alarm rather than amuse, and argues that its rescue qualifiers drift toward circularity, so the theory is presented as another instructive near-miss rather than a settled answer.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage surveys the three classical families, shows each failing against counterexamples, superiority against victimless puns, relief against circularity, incongruity against alarming mismatches, then proposes that humor may be a family-resemblance concept without a single shared essence, while insisting in the final paragraph that each theory maps a genuine region and that general knowledge, short of a one-line formula, remains available.
explanations.C: This choice elevates the superiority theory's premise into the passage's thesis. The second paragraph presents the uncharitable-pleasure view as the oldest family's claim and then dismantles it with victimless humor, warm laughter at one's own blunders, so the passage cannot be endorsing it as fundamental.
explanations.D: This choice converts the passage's measured skepticism into a blanket denial it explicitly rejects. The final paragraph states that none of this licenses the conclusion that nothing general can be said, crediting each theory within its region, so radical cultural variation is not treated as fatal to all theorizing.

questions.stem: The primary function of the fourth paragraph's examples of the stranger at the door and the discrepant bank balance is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: show that incongruity alone cannot be sufficient, since some perceived mismatches unsettle or alarm rather than amuse.

choices.label: B
choices.text: illustrate situations in which the superiority theory clearly outperforms the incongruity theory as an account of amusement.

choices.label: C
choices.text: demonstrate that financial anxieties are a richer source of comedy than domestic surprises.

choices.label: D
choices.text: prove that expectations play no role in the experience of amusement.

explanations.A: Correct. Both examples present genuine incongruities, an expectation sharply violated by what arrives, that produce unease or alarm rather than laughter, establishing that the mere perception of mismatch cannot suffice for humor and thereby motivating the qualifiers, resolved, playful, benign, whose circularity the paragraph goes on to expose.
explanations.B: This choice imports a comparison the paragraph does not stage. Neither example involves triumph over a defeated party, so neither offers material on which the superiority theory could outperform anything; the examples test sufficiency of incongruity, not the rivalry between families.
explanations.C: This choice treats the examples as claims about comic subject matter, when they function as counterexamples to a definition. The paragraph offers them precisely as incongruities that are not funny, so no ranking of comedic sources is in play.
explanations.D: This choice reverses the examples' logic. Both depend on expectations being violated, which presupposes that expectation is central to incongruity; the point is that violated expectation is not enough for amusement, not that it is irrelevant to amusement.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the relief theory is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: wholly dismissive, since the theory's outdated hydraulic science leaves it with nothing of value to contribute.

choices.label: B
choices.text: fully persuaded, since the theory alone explains why laughter is social and contagious.

choices.label: C
choices.text: uncertain, since the passage declines to evaluate the theory pending further empirical evidence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: discriminating, crediting its feel for timing and release while judging its psychology obsolete and its explanations circular.

explanations.A: This choice erases the credit the paragraph explicitly extends. The relief theory is said to retain an insight the others lack, its grasp of timing and the eruption of laughter at the release of built-up tension, so the author's verdict is mixed rather than wholly dismissive.
explanations.B: This choice attaches the wrong evidence to the wrong theory. The social and contagious character of laughter is discussed in the sixth paragraph's social-signal account, not credited to relief theory, and the passage endorses no theory fully.
explanations.C: This choice mistakes a delivered judgment for a suspended one. The paragraph renders a clear assessment, hydraulics failed as science, detailed explanations turn circular, timing insight remains, rather than deferring evaluation to future evidence.
explanations.D: Correct. The third paragraph credits relief theory with a genuine and distinctive insight into comic timing, the eruption of laughter when suspense resolves into harmlessness, while noting that its hydraulic psychology has not survived as science and that, pressed for detail, it explains amusing releases only as releases of the amusing kind, a discriminating mixture of credit and criticism.

questions.stem: The phrase family-resemblance concept, as used in the fifth paragraph, most nearly describes a concept whose instances:

choices.label: A
choices.text: are inherited biologically, in the way that physical traits pass between generations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: can be ranked from most to least central according to a single underlying measure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: overlap in various shifting similarities without any one feature shared by all of them.

choices.label: D
choices.text: share exactly one essential property that reliably distinguishes them from all non-instances of the concept.

explanations.A: This choice literalizes the family metaphor into biological inheritance. The paragraph's point concerns the logical structure of a concept, resemblances that crisscross without a common thread, not any mechanism by which funny things beget other funny things.
explanations.B: This choice substitutes a graded structure with a single measure for the structure the passage describes. A single underlying measure is precisely the kind of unifying feature the family-resemblance picture denies; the resemblances run along various dimensions rather than one scale.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph explains that instances of humor may overlap and crisscross like the members of a family, alike in various ways, without one feature running through the whole, which is exactly the structure of similarity without a universally shared property, and it is why every proposed essence must fail somewhere on so heterogeneous a terrain.
explanations.D: This choice states the essentialist picture the phrase is introduced to reject. A single essential property shared by all instances is what the three classical theories sought and what the family-resemblance proposal denies exists for humor.

questions.stem: At a memorial gathering, a speaker's story about the deceased's habit of mislabeling household leftovers draws sustained, affectionate laughter from the family, releasing the accumulated tension of the ceremony without mocking its subject. Based on the passage, this episode would create the most difficulty for:

choices.label: A
choices.text: a strict superiority theorist, since the warm laughter elevates the laughers over no defeated party.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a relief theorist, since no tension of any kind precedes the laughter.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the social-signal account, since the laughter occurs among strangers who share no assumptions about the deceased.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the family-resemblance view, since the episode proves that all humor shares a single essence.

explanations.A: Correct. The laughter is explicitly affectionate and directed at a beloved figure's harmless quirk, supplying no victim and no consciousness of eminence, which is the precise configuration the second paragraph uses against superiority theory; meanwhile the ceremony's accumulated tension and its release fit the relief theory's distinctive insight into timing, so the episode embarrasses the former while suiting the latter.
explanations.B: This choice misdescribes the scenario, which specifies the accumulated tension of a memorial ceremony being released. Far from lacking tension, the episode is nearly a textbook case for the relief theory's strength as the passage presents it.
explanations.C: This choice misdescribes the participants. The laughter arises within a family sharing a fund of memories and assumptions about the deceased, which is fertile ground for the social-signal account, shared laughter marking shared assumptions, not a difficulty for it.
explanations.D: This choice misunderstands what would trouble the family-resemblance view. That view is threatened by the discovery of a single essence uniting all humor, and one warm anecdote at a memorial establishes nothing of the kind; the episode is simply another instance for the crisscrossing terrain.

questions.stem: Which of the following situations is most analogous to the circularity the passage attributes to defenders of the incongruity theory who add the qualifier benign?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A geologist classifies rocks by hardness using a standardized scratch test applied identically to every sample.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A historian revises a chronology after the discovery of documents that contradict the accepted sequence of events.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A chef adjusts a recipe repeatedly until tasters can no longer reliably distinguish the result from the original dish.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A critic defines great literature as writing possessing greatness and, asked what greatness is, says it is what great works possess.

explanations.A: This choice describes an independent operational test, a scratch procedure whose outcome does not presuppose the classification it supports. The benign qualifier's defect is exactly the absence of such independence, so a standardized external test is the opposite of the analogy sought.
explanations.B: This choice describes ordinary self-correction in the face of new evidence, a virtue of inquiry rather than a defect of definition. Nothing in the historian's revision defines a term by means of itself.
explanations.C: This choice describes iterative refinement toward a matching target, with tasters supplying an independent criterion of success. The process may be trial and error, but it is not circular, since the standard being met does not restate the thing being defined.
explanations.D: Correct. The critic's definition presupposes the very quality it is supposed to explain, exactly as benign, in the incongruity theory's usage, threatens to mean nothing more determinate than such as to amuse, so the theory explains the funny by the funny; both are definitions that smuggle the explanandum into the explanation.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the fifth paragraph's suggestion that humor is a family-resemblance concept lacking any single defining essence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that professional comedians disagree sharply and persistently with one another about which of their own routines are funniest and why particular audiences respond to them.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A precisely specified cognitive condition, defined independently of amusement, present in essentially every instance of humor and absent from matched non-cases.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that laughter occurs in some social species other than humans.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that audiences laugh more readily and more loudly in groups than when watching the same material alone.

explanations.A: This choice reports evaluative disagreement about degrees of funniness, which is compatible with either picture of humor's structure. Experts can disagree about rankings whether or not a single essence underlies the category, so the finding cannot adjudicate the definitional question.
explanations.B: Correct. The family-resemblance suggestion rests on the claim that no single property runs through all funny things and only funny things, which is why every proposed essence meets counterexamples. A condition defined independently of amusement, thereby escaping the benign-style circularity, found present in essentially all humor and absent from matched non-humorous cases including alarming incongruities, would be exactly the universal essence the paragraph says does not exist.
explanations.C: This choice extends laughter's range across species without bearing on whether human humor shares one essence. Cross-species laughter is equally compatible with an essentialist or a family-resemblance account of the concept's structure.
explanations.D: This choice supports the sixth paragraph's social-signal observations rather than addressing the definitional question. Group facilitation of laughter says nothing about whether a single property unites all instances of humor.
